does: This paper introduces Cordyceps, a data poisoning attack that teaches language models an information hiding scheme through semantic associations between shared knowledge and attacker-controlled phrases. Unlike traditional poisoning attacks relying on fixed triggers, the method enables covert control where arbitrary malicious instructions can be encoded and decoded through natural semantic associations. The authors characterize these attacks formally and evaluate them across five LLMs and seven defenses, demonstrating that covert control attacks achieve approximately 40% higher success rates than heuristic prompt injection attacks while circumventing detection-based and fine-tuning defenses with minimal poisoned data.
connects: This work extends the literature on data poisoning attacks for LLMs, building on prior research into backdoor vulnerabilities in neural networks and trigger-based poisoning methods. It responds to limitations of existing approaches like fixed-trigger backdoors that are vulnerable to outlier detection and clean-data regularization. The paper also connects to work on prompt injection and adversarial robustness in language models, offering a more subtle attack vector that exploits semantic properties rather than syntactic triggers. It engages with the broader concern about fine-tuning on uncurated internet-scale data and the trustworthiness of supply chains in LLM development.
enables: This research opens new directions for understanding and defending against semantic-level poisoning attacks in language models. It motivates the development of more sophisticated defenses that can detect information hiding schemes rather than relying on syntactic pattern matching or outlier detection. The work raises important questions about how to safely fine-tune models on untrusted data and whether semantic associations can be monitored or sanitized. Future work could explore how covert control generalizes across model architectures and training procedures, develop stronger defenses against information-hiding-based attacks, and investigate the theoretical limits of detecting such subtle poisoning schemes.
